*   **Building the Wrong Thing:** By trying to anticipate future needs, you often guess wrong. You spend time building flexibility that isn’t used. By month 6, you have swathes of code in the codebase that aren’t actually providing value but make the system harder to understand and change. It’s like paying interest on a loan you didn’t need to take. One programmer wryly titled a post *“The best code I ever wrote was code I deleted,”* referring to thousands of lines of “enterprise-grade” abstraction that turned out to be unnecessary.
*   **Stifled Iteration:** Early in a project, requirements can change rapidly with user feedback. Rigid architectures (with many layers or patterns) can make it difficult to pivot. Developers might find that making a change requires modifying multiple classes and interfaces, updating documentation for a framework they built, or worrying about breaking some generic system. This slows down the ability to respond to user needs – the opposite of what a young product requires.
*   **Cognitive Overhead:** For a solo dev or small team, understanding an overly abstract codebase is tough. When you return to a piece of code 9 months later, and it’s littered with design patterns (that perhaps only *you* use in this unusual way), you may struggle to remember how it all fits. New team members, similarly, will be lost trying to decipher your homemade framework. Simpler code might be easier for your future self or colleagues to pick up and extend.
*   **Papering Over Lack of Clarity:** Sometimes we reach for abstractions when we’re not entirely sure what we’re building; it’s a way to delay concrete decisions. But by 6–12 months, this can result in a contrived structure that actually *increases* technical debt – you have to maintain the abstraction *and* the actual feature. You might end up needing to unravel or simplify the design to add real features.

**Better Alternative:** **YAGNI (You Aren’t Gonna Need It)** is a key principle to embrace. Build only what you need **for the immediate requirements** and defer generalization until you have evidence that multiple use cases require it. In practice:

*   **Start with Specific Implementations:** Write code to solve the problem at hand. If you need a particular feature, implement it in a straightforward way first.
*   **Refactor When Needed, Not Before:** When you find yourself needing a second or third similar feature, *then* it might be time to abstract or generalize. This way, your abstraction is informed by real needs rather than guesswork.
*   **Use High-Level Frameworks Judiciously:** Rather than writing your own overarching architecture, use well-known frameworks that implement best practices (but only as much as needed). For example, instead of a custom plugin system, see if your framework has an add-on system or simply use good modular code practices.
*   **Focus on Clear Code:** Early on, optimize for clarity and simplicity. This means flat and simple code organization, clear naming, and avoiding indirection. A common rule: if explaining a piece of code involves saying “we have this factory that produces strategy objects based on a context which are then decorated by…” for a simple feature, it’s over-engineered.

**Warning Signs:**

*   You’re spending a lot of time **writing code that doesn’t deliver user-facing value**, e.g., a complex subsystem that a user won’t even notice if it’s there or not.
*   You find yourself simulating a large company architecture: e.g., building a mini “service layer” or elaborate micro-kernel plugin system when you’re the only developer.
*   You have **abstraction layers that have only one implementation** (a telltale sign of YAGNI violation – you made it abstract “just in case” but there’s only one thing there).
*   Significant percentage of your code is framework-like, rather than product features.
*   You talk about things like *“for future extensibility”* or *“in case we need to swap out X”* but you have no concrete requirement to do so yet.

**Verdict & Example:** **The simplest design that meets your current needs is almost always best for an indie project.** Over-anticipating future needs often leads to building features that solve imaginary problems. This becomes dead weight (**“structural tech debt”** that must be maintained or worked around). A classic example is a developer who, inspired by enterprise architecture, created an extremely abstract data processing pipeline in an app’s first month – complete with plugins, adapters, and dynamic loading. It turned out the app never needed to support multiple interchangeable data sources as originally thought. The result was a convoluted system that confused other developers and slowed down feature development. At month 9, facing stagnation, he stripped out the generic layers, simplifying to a single pipeline tailored to the only data source the app ever used. The immediate outcome was a drastic reduction in code (deleting over 2,000 lines) and a faster pace of adding features.

As another real-world insight, consider **the “90/10 rule” of complexity** echoed in industry: 90% of the benefits often come from simpler 10% of the effort. For instance, many companies realized that a **“modular monolith” gives 90% of the benefits of microservices with 10% of the complexity** – this principle extends to many design decisions. The key takeaway is to **favor straightforward designs** and evolve them when needed, rather than trying to predict the future. Your future self would much rather refactor one simple codebase than maintain a constellation of unnecessary complexity built in the name of future-proofing. [\[byteiota.com\]](https://byteiota.com/microservices-rollback-2026-42-return-to-monoliths/)

***

## **Conclusion: Embrace Simplicity and Pragmatism Early**

In the critical first year of a solo or small-team software project, **time-to-market and adaptability are paramount**. Each of the above patterns shows a common theme: **decisions that optimize for an imagined future (or mimic large-scale practices) often hurt indie developers in the present.** The cost is paid in reduced development speed, harder maintenance, and sometimes direct technical failures or expenses down the line.

**Key Lessons Recap (tl;dr):**

*   **Use simple, proven tools unless a compelling need justifies complexity.** For databases, that’s usually relational SQL (with JSON flexibility if needed) over trendy NoSQL for most apps. For hosting, that’s a single server or PaaS over a self-built distributed infrastructure. For architecture, it’s a monolith over microservices. [\[indiehackers.com\]](https://www.indiehackers.com/post/why-i-had-to-migrate-from-mongodb-to-postgresql-47d089b941) [\[indiehackers.com\]](https://www.indiehackers.com/post/how-do-you-host-your-saas-eb894f83ea) [\[byteiota.com\]](https://byteiota.com/microservices-rollback-2026-42-return-to-monoliths/)
*   **Beware of “industry best practices” when taken out of context.** What works for Google or Netflix will likely sink a one-person project. Scale *dictates* architecture; don’t copy architectures built for millions of users when you have zero.
*   **Third-party services are your friend (for auth, payments, infrastructure).** Free open-source is great, but your time isn’t free. Offload undifferentiated heavy lifting—like authentication—so you can focus on your unique value. [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/userfront/when-has-rolling-your-own-auth-gone-wrong-3lak)
*   **Defer complexity.** Whether it’s state management, real-time updates, or fancy patterns, wait until the simpler approach truly breaks down. Many indie devs regret not following the “You Aren’t Gonna Need It” rule sooner.
*   **Watch for red flags.** We’ve highlighted warning signs for each pattern – these are essentially the *smoke that precedes a tech-debt fire*. Pay attention to them. If you catch yourself in one of these patterns, consider a course-correction *before* it’s too costly.

In summary, the most painful tech debts for small projects often come from **choices that were too “big” for the project’s britches** – tools or architectures that introduce more overhead than the value they provide at small scale. The most successful solo developers often stick to simpler tech stacks and pragmatic solutions, adding complexity only as a *response* to real scaling pains, not in *anticipation* of them. This approach keeps them nimble in the early days, allowing them to iterate quickly and focus on product-market fit – which, ultimately, is the true challenge for any indie project.

By learning from these common patterns and the experiences of those who’ve lived through them, you can avoid writing a post-mortem titled *“We spent a year building the wrong tech stack.”* Instead, you can spend that year building features and delighting users – and deal with scaling the tech when it’s a welcomed problem to have.

***

*   ORBITECH article, *“ORM vs Raw SQL: Why Your Database Queries Are Secretly Sabotaging You”* – Discusses performance pitfalls of ORMs, including a real case of an ORM generating a debilitating 1,200-line SQL query. [\[orbitech-a...cademy.com\]](https://orbitech-ai-academy.com/au/formules/databases/university/orm-vs-raw-sql-why-your-database-queries-are-secretly-sabotaging-you-orbitech), [\[orbitech-a...cademy.com\]](https://orbitech-ai-academy.com/au/formules/databases/university/orm-vs-raw-sql-why-your-database-queries-are-secretly-sabotaging-you-orbitech)

Each of these sources provides further context and examples of the themes discussed, reinforcing the guidance to help you steer clear of these common early-stage tech missteps. Remember, as a solo or small-team developer, **the greatest asset you have is speed and agility**. Choosing technologies that preserve those qualities – by staying simple and focusing on immediate needs – is often the key to avoiding painful technical debt down the road. Good luck, and *happy coding* – with fewer regrets! [\[byteiota.com\]](https://byteiota.com/microservices-rollback-2026-42-return-to-monoliths/), [\[indiehackers.com\]](https://www.indiehackers.com/post/how-do-you-host-your-saas-eb894f83ea)
